sourceQuestion:
A disk has 8 equidistant tracks. The diameters of the innermost and outermost tracks are 2 cm and 8 cm respectively. The innermost track has a storage capacity of 10 MB. What is the total amount of data that can be stored on the disk if it is used with a drive that rotates it with Constant Linear Velocity (CLV)?

A. 1.0040 MB <= 80 MB
B. 1.0040 MB <= 80 MB
C. 1.0040 MB <= 360 MB
D. 1.0040 MB <= 360 MB

A. 2040 MB
B. 1.0040 MB <= 80 MB
C. 1.0040 MB <= 360 MB
D. 1.0040 MB <= 360 MB

parsed.stem: A disk has 8 equidistant tracks. The diameters of the innermost and outermost tracks are 2 cm and 8 cm respectively. The innermost track has a storage capacity of 10 MB. What is the total amount of data that can be stored on the disk if it is used with a drive that rotates it with Constant Linear Velocity (CLV)?

sourceQuestion:
A disk has 8 equidistant tracks. The diameters of the innermost and outermost tracks are 2 cm and 8 cm respectively. The innermost track has a storage capacity of 10 MB. The disk has 16 sectors per track and is currently at the end of the 3rd sector of the innermost track. The head can move at a speed of 1 meter/second. How much time would it take to read the entire innermost track?

A. 13.5 mu s
B. 10 mu s
C. 9.5 mu s
D. 20 mu s

parsed.stem: A disk has 8 equidistant tracks. The diameters of the innermost and outermost tracks are 2 cm and 8 cm respectively. The innermost track has a storage capacity of 10 MB. The disk has 16 sectors per track and is currently at the end of the 3rd sector of the innermost track. The head can move at a speed of 1 meter/second. How much time would it take to read the entire innermost track?

sourceQuestion:
A hard disk system has the following parameters:
- Number of tracks = 500
- Number of sectors/track = 100
- Number of bytes/sector = 500
- Time taken by the head to move from one track to adjacent track = 1 ms
- Rotation speed = 600 rpm

What is the average time taken for transferring 250 bytes from the disk?

A. 300.5 ms
B. 255.5 ms
C. 255 ms
D. 300 ms

parsed.stem:
A hard disk system has the following parameters:
- Number of tracks = 500
- Number of sectors/track = 100
- Number of bytes/sector = 500
- Time taken by the head to move from one track to adjacent track = 1 ms
- Rotation speed = 600 rpm

What is the average time taken for transferring 250 bytes from the disk?

sourceQuestion:
The correct matching for the following pairs is:

(A) Disk Scheduling    (1) Round robin
(B) Batch Processing   (2) SCAN
(C) Thrashing          (3) LRU
(D) Interrupt processing (4) FIFO

A. A-3 B-4 C-2 D-1
B. A-4 B-3 C-2 D-1
C. A-2 B-4 C-1 D-3
D. A-3 B-4 C-3 D-2

parsed.stem: The correct matching for the following pairs is:

options.text: Interrupt processing (4) FIFO A. A-3 B-4 C-2 D-1 B. A-4 B-3 C-2 D-1 C. A-2 B-4 C-1 D-3 D. A-3 B-4 C-3 D-2

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following disk scheduling strategies is likely to give the best throughput?
A. Farthest cylinder next
B. Nearest cylinder next
C. First come first served
D. Elevator algorithm

parsed.stem: Which of the following disk scheduling strategies is likely to give the best throughput?

sourceQuestion:
Consider an operating system capable of loading and executing a single sequential user process at a time. The disk head scheduling algorithm used is First Come First Served (FCFS). If FCFS is replaced by Shortest Seek Time First (SSTF), claimed by the vendor to give 50% better benchmark results, what is the expected improvement in I/O performance of user programs?

A. 50%
B. 40%
C. 25%
D. 0%

parsed.stem: Consider an operating system capable of loading and executing a single sequential user process at a time. The disk head scheduling algorithm used is First Come First Served (FCFS). If FCFS is replaced by Shortest Seek Time First (SSTF), claimed by the vendor to give 50% better benchmark results, what is the expected improvement in I/O performance of user programs?

sourceQuestion:
Consider a disk system with 100 cylinders. The requests to access the cylinders occur in following sequence:
4, 31, 10, 17, 19, 73, 2, 15, 6, 20
Assuming that the head is currently at cylinder 50, what is the time taken to satisfy all requests if it takes 1 ms to move from one cylinder to adjacent one and Shortest Seek Time First (SSTF) policy is used?

A. 95 ms
B. 119 ms
C. 233 ms
D. 276 ms

parsed.stem:
Consider a disk system with 100 cylinders. The requests to access the cylinders occur in following sequence:
4, 31, 10, 17, 19, 73, 2, 15, 6, 20
Assuming that the head is currently at cylinder 50, what is the time taken to satisfy all requests if it takes 1 ms to move from one cylinder to adjacent one and Shortest Seek Time First (SSTF) policy is used?

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following five disk access requests of the form (request id, cylinder number) that are present in the disk scheduler queue at a given time:
(P, 165), (Q, 85), (R, 110), (S, 30), (T, 115)
The head is currently at cylinder 100. The scheduler uses the LOOK algorithm moving towards larger cylinder numbers first. The order in which the requests will be serviced is:

A. P, T, R, Q, S
B. T, P, R, Q, S
C. R, T, P, Q, S
D. R, P, T, Q, S
sourceAnswer: C. R, T, P, Q, S

parsed.stem:
Consider the following five disk access requests of the form (request id, cylinder number) that are present in the disk scheduler queue at a given time:
(P, 165), (Q, 85), (R, 110), (S, 30), (T, 115)
The head is currently at cylinder 100. The scheduler uses the LOOK algorithm moving towards larger cylinder numbers first. The order in which the requests will be serviced is:

sourceQuestion:
A disk has 200 tracks (numbered 0 through 199). At a given time, it was servicing the request of reading data from track 120, and at the previous request, service was for track 90. The pending requests (in order of their arrival) are for track numbers:
30, 75, 115, 130, 110, 80, 20, 25
How many times will the head change its direction for the disk scheduling policies SSTF (Shortest Seek Time First) and FCFS (First Come First Serve) respectively?
A. 2 and 3
B. 3 and 3
C. 3 and 4
D. 4 and 6

parsed.stem:
A disk has 200 tracks (numbered 0 through 199). At a given time, it was servicing the request of reading data from track 120, and at the previous request, service was for track 90. The pending requests (in order of their arrival) are for track numbers:
30, 75, 115, 130, 110, 80, 20, 25
How many times will the head change its direction for the disk scheduling policies SSTF (Shortest Seek Time First) and FCFS (First Come First Serve) respectively?

The head of a hard disk serves requests following the shortest seek time first (SSTF) policy. The head is initially positioned at track number 100. Which of the following request sets will cause the head to change its direction after servicing every request, assuming that the head does not change direction if there is a request at the same distance in both directions?

sourceQuestion:
The head of a hard disk serves requests following the shortest seek time first (SSTF) policy. What is the maximum cardinality of the request set, so that the head changes its direction after servicing every request, if the total number of tracks are 2048 and the head can start from any track?
A. 9
B. 10
C. 11
D. 12

parsed.stem: The head of a hard disk serves requests following the shortest seek time first (SSTF) policy. What is the maximum cardinality of the request set, so that the head changes its direction after servicing every request, if the total number of tracks are 2048 and the head can start from any track?

sourceQuestion:
In the index allocation scheme of blocks to a file, the maximum possible size of the file depends on
A. the size of the blocks, and the size of the address of the blocks.
B. the number of blocks used for the index, and the size of the blocks.
C. the size of the blocks, the number of blocks used for the index, and the size of the address of the blocks.
D. None of the above.
sourceAnswer: C. the size of the blocks, the number of blocks used for the index, and the size of the address of the blocks.

parsed.stem: In the index allocation scheme of blocks to a file, the maximum possible size of the file depends on

options.text: the size of the blocks, and the size of the address of the blocks.
the number of blocks used for the index, and the size of the blocks.
the size of the blocks, the number of blocks used for the index, and the size of the address of the blocks.

sourceQuestion:
The data blocks of a very large file in the Unix file system are allocated using
A. contiguous allocation
B. linked allocation
C. indexed allocation
D. an extension of indexed allocation
sourceAnswer: D. an extension of indexed allocation

parsed.stem: The data blocks of a very large file in the Unix file system are allocated using

sourceQuestion:
In a file allocation system, which of the following allocation scheme(s) can be used if no external fragmentation is allowed?
1. Contiguous
2. Linked
3. Indexed
A. 1 and 3 only
B. 1 only
C. 3 only
D. 2 and 3 only

parsed.stem:
In a file allocation system, which of the following allocation scheme(s) can be used if no external fragmentation is allowed?
1. Contiguous
2. Linked
3. Indexed

sourceQuestion:
Consider a linear list based directory implementation in a file system. Each directory is a list of nodes, where each node contains the file name along with the file metadata, such as the list of pointers to the data blocks. Consider a given directory foo. Which of the following operations will necessarily require scanning the entire list of nodes of foo?

A. Creation of a new file in foo
B. Deletion of an existing file from foo
C. Renaming of an existing file in foo
D. Opening of an existing file in foo

parsed.stem: Consider a linear list based directory implementation in a file system. Each directory is a list of nodes, where each node contains the file name along with the file metadata, such as the list of pointers to the data blocks. Consider a given directory foo. Which of the following operations will necessarily require scanning the entire list of nodes of foo?

options.text: Creation of a new file in foo
Deletion of an existing file from foo
Renaming of an existing file in foo
Opening of an existing file in foo

sourceQuestion:
In a particular Unix OS, each data block is of size 1024 bytes, each inode has 10 direct data block addresses and three additional addresses: one for single indirect block, one for double indirect block and one for triple indirect block. Also, each block can contain addresses for 128 blocks. Which one of the following is approximately the maximum file size?

A. 512 MB
B. 2 GB
C. 8 GB
D. 16 GB

parsed.stem: In a particular Unix OS, each data block is of size 1024 bytes, each inode has 10 direct data block addresses and three additional addresses: one for single indirect block, one for double indirect block and one for triple indirect block. Also, each block can contain addresses for 128 blocks. Which one of the following is approximately the maximum file size?

sourceAnswer: D. x = 5 and y = 15 and the virtual address of a in the parent and child are the same

options.text: x = 10 and y = 15 and the virtual address of a in the parent and child are the same
x = 5 and y = 15 and the virtual address of a in the parent and child are different
x = 5 and y = 15 and the virtual address of a in the parent and child are the same

sourceQuestion:
A process executes the following code
for(i=0; i<n; i++) fork();
The total number of child processes created is
A. n
B. 2^n - 1
C. 2^n
D. 2^(n-1) - 1

Which one or more of the following options guarantee that a computer system will transition from user mode to kernel mode?
A. Function Call
B. malloc Call
C. Page Fault
D. System Call

parsed.stem: Which one or more of the following options guarantee that a computer system will transition from user mode to kernel mode?

sourceQuestion:
A process executes the following segment of code:

for (i = 1; i <= n; i++)
    fork();

The number of new processes created is
A. n
B. n(n+1)/2
C. 2^n - 1
D. 3^n - 1

Which of the following is an example of spooled device?
A. A line printer used to print the output of a number of jobs
B. A terminal used to enter input data to a running program
C. A secondary storage device in a virtual memory system
D. A graphic display device
sourceAnswer: A. A line printer used to print the output of a number of jobs

parsed.stem: Which of the following is an example of spooled device?

options.text: A line printer used to print the output of a number of jobs
A terminal used to enter input data to a running program
A secondary storage device in a virtual memory system

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following is an example of a spooled device?
A. The terminal used to enter the input data for the C program being executed
B. An output device used to print the output of a number of jobs
C. The secondary memory device in a virtual storage system
D. The swapping area on a disk used by the swapper
sourceAnswer: B. An output device used to print the output of a number of jobs

parsed.stem: Which of the following is an example of a spooled device?

options.text: The terminal used to enter the input data for the C program being executed
An output device used to print the output of a number of jobs
The secondary memory device in a virtual storage system
The swapping area on a disk used by the swapper

sourceQuestion:
Which one of the following is true for a CPU having a single interrupt request line and a single interrupt grant line?
A. Neither vectored interrupt nor multiple interrupting devices are possible
B. Vectored interrupts are not possible but multiple interrupting devices are possible
C. Vectored interrupts are possible but multiple interrupting devices are not possible
D. Both vectored interrupts and multiple interrupting devices are possible
sourceAnswer: D. Both vectored interrupts and multiple interrupting devices are possible

parsed.stem: Which one of the following is true for a CPU having a single interrupt request line and a single interrupt grant line?

options.text: Neither vectored interrupt nor multiple interrupting devices are possible
Vectored interrupts are not possible but multiple interrupting devices are possible
Vectored interrupts are possible but multiple interrupting devices are not possible
Both vectored interrupts and multiple interrupting devices are possible

sourceQuestion:
Typically, user programs are prevented from handling I/O directly by I/O instructions in them. For CPUs having explicit I/O instructions, such I/O protection is ensured by having the I/O instruction privileged. In a CPU with memory mapped I/O, there is no explicit I/O instruction. Which one of the following is true for a CPU with memory mapped I/O?
A. I/O protection is ensured by operating system routine(s)
B. I/O protection is ensured by a hardware trap
C. I/O protection is ensured during system configuration
D. I/O protection is not possible
sourceAnswer: A. I/O protection is ensured by operating system routine(s)

parsed.stem: Typically, user programs are prevented from handling I/O directly by I/O instructions in them. For CPUs having explicit I/O instructions, such I/O protection is ensured by having the I/O instruction privileged. In a CPU with memory mapped I/O, there is no explicit I/O instruction. Which one of the following is true for a CPU with memory mapped I/O?

options.text: I/O protection is ensured by operating system routine(s)
I/O protection is ensured by a hardware trap
I/O protection is ensured during system configuration

sourceQuestion:
The following are some events that occur after a device controller issues an interrupt while process L is under execution.
P. The processor pushes the process status of L onto the control stack
Q. The processor finishes the execution of the current instruction
R. The processor executes the interrupt service routine
S. The processor pops the process status of L from the control stack
T. The processor loads the new PC value based on the interrupt

Which of the following is the correct order in which the above events occur?
A. QTPRS
B. PTRSQ
C. TRPQS
D. QTPRS

parsed.stem:
The following are some events that occur after a device controller issues an interrupt while process L is under execution.
P. The processor pushes the process status of L onto the control stack
Q. The processor finishes the execution of the current instruction
R. The processor executes the interrupt service routine
S. The processor pops the process status of L from the control stack
T. The processor loads the new PC value based on the interrupt

Which of the following is the correct order in which the above events occur?

sourceQuestion:
What is the bit rate of a video terminal unit with 80 characters/line, 8 bits/character, and a horizontal sweep time of 100 microseconds (including 20 microseconds of retrace time)?
A. 6.4 Mbps
B. 0.8 Mbps
C. 0.8 Mbps
D. 0.64 Mbps

parsed.stem: What is the bit rate of a video terminal unit with 80 characters/line, 8 bits/character, and a horizontal sweep time of 100 microseconds (including 20 microseconds of retrace time)?

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following DMA transfer modes and interrupt handling mechanisms will enable the highest I/O bandwidth?
A. Transparent DMA and Polling interrupts
B. Cycle-stealing and Vectored interrupts
C. Block transfer and Vectored interrupts
D. Block transfer and Polling interrupts
sourceAnswer: C. Block transfer and Vectored interrupts

parsed.stem: Which of the following DMA transfer modes and interrupt handling mechanisms will enable the highest I/O bandwidth?

sourceQuestion:
I/O redirection
A. implies changing the name of a file
B. can be employed to use an existing file as input file for a program
C. implies connecting programs through a pipe
D. None of the above
sourceAnswer: B. can be employed to use an existing file as input file for a program

parsed.stem: I/O redirection

options.text: implies changing the name of a file
can be employed to use an existing file as input file for a program
implies connecting programs through a pipe
